AP Gov students must know that prior restraint is not absolutely prohibited — national security emergencies could potentially justify it — but the bar is nearly impossible to clear.

AP Gov Unit 4 connects the gender gap to political socialization (socialization into gender roles), policy differences (women more supportive of social programs and gun control), and the role of realignment in shaping group-based party coalitions.

questions.explanation: Political efficacy measures citizens' belief in their ability to understand politics (internal efficacy) and their belief that government is responsive to them (external efficacy). AP Gov Unit 4 links efficacy to political socialization (family and education build internal efficacy) and trust in government (eroded external efficacy since Vietnam and Watergate); low external efficacy is a major driver of political cynicism and disengagement.

questions.explanation: Scientific polling uses probability-based random sampling to estimate the opinions of a large population from a relatively small sample — typically 1,000–1,500 respondents for national surveys, with a margin of error around ±3%. Key validity threats include question wording bias, response bias, and the 'Bradley effect' (social desirability). AP Gov Unit 4 distinguishes scientific polls from push polls, straw polls, and convenience samples; the exit poll, tracking poll, benchmark poll, and flash poll are all subtypes students should recognize.

questions.explanation: Opinion polling uses structured surveys to measure the distribution of public attitudes on candidates, policies, and institutions. questions.explanation: A random sample is a subset of a population in which every individual has an equal and known probability of being selected, making it possible to generalize results to the whole population within a quantifiable margin of error. questions.explanation: Libertarianism is a political ideology that prioritizes individual freedom above all else and opposes government intervention in both economic matters (free markets, low taxes) and personal life (social freedoms, civil liberties). The Libertarian Party has run presidential candidates since 1972; Gary Johnson won 3.3% in 2016. It was the foundation of Reaganomics (Reagan's 1981 Economic Recovery Tax Act cut top income tax rates from 70% to 50%, then to 28% in 1986) and the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act. AP Gov Unit 4 contrasts it with Keynesian demand-side economics and the ongoing debate over tax policy and income inequality.

FDR's New Deal implemented Keynesian principles; the 2009 American Recovery and Reinvestment Act ($831 billion stimulus) was explicitly Keynesian. AP Gov Unit 4 contrasts Keynesian demand-side with supply-side economics and connects it to debates about the role of government in the economy.

The Fed — an independent agency created by the Federal Reserve Act of 1913 — targets 2% inflation and maximum employment. AP Gov Unit 4 connects monetary policy to the debate over Federal Reserve independence, contrasts it with congressional fiscal policy (taxing and spending), and notes the Fed's role as an 'iron triangle' bureaucratic actor.

questions.explanation: The Electoral College is the constitutional mechanism — Article II, Section 1 and the 12th Amendment — by which the president and vice president are elected. Each state has electors equal to its congressional delegation (538 total); a candidate needs 270 to win. Most states use winner-take-all allocation. Four presidents have won without a popular vote majority (1824, 1876, 1888, 2000, 2016). AP Gov Unit 5 connects the Electoral College to federalism, campaign strategy (battleground states), and ongoing reform debates — the National Popular Vote Interstate Compact being a notable alternative.

questions.explanation: Winner-take-all (or first-past-the-post) is the U.S. electoral rule in which the candidate with the most votes wins the entire contest — a House seat, a state's Electoral College votes, etc. — regardless of vote share. It structurally advantages a two-party system (Duverger's Law) because rational voters abandon third parties to avoid 'wasting' their vote. AP Gov Unit 5 connects winner-take-all to the dominance of the Democratic and Republican parties, the persistence of the two-party system, and why proportional representation (used in most democracies) produces multi-party systems.

The New Deal coalition (1932 Democrats) and the Southern realignment (1964–1994, as white Southerners shifted to the Republican Party after the Civil Rights Act) are the canonical AP Gov examples. They 'link' the public to policymakers. AP Gov Unit 5 treats all four as interconnected: parties aggregate interests, elections translate preferences into representation, interest groups lobby between elections, and media shapes the agenda. questions.explanation: Mass media — newspapers, television, radio, and digital platforms — are linkage institutions that inform citizens about politics, set the agenda (agenda-setting theory), frame issues (framing effect), and prime voters about which issues to use to evaluate politicians (priming theory). The shift from 'broadcast era' (network dominance 1950s–90s) to 'cable and internet era' has increased partisan sorting — Fox News, MSNBC, and social media echo chambers. questions.explanation: A political action committee (PAC) is an organization that raises and spends money to elect or defeat candidates. Traditional PACs are limited to $5,000/candidate/election in direct contributions. Super PACs — created after Citizens United v. FEC (2010) and SpeechNow.org v. FEC (2010) — can raise and spend unlimited amounts for 'independent expenditures' but cannot coordinate directly with campaigns. FEC (2010), decided 5-4 with Justice Kennedy writing, ruled that the First Amendment prohibits the government from restricting political expenditures by corporations, associations, or unions as independent political speech. The ruling invalidated Section 203 of the Bipartisan Campaign Reform Act (McCain-Feingold, 2002). It opened the door to Super PACs and dramatically increased outside spending in elections. McCain-Feingold (BCRA, 2002) banned soft money to parties; Citizens United (2010) enabled Super PACs. questions.topic: Unit 5: Political Participation · Campaign Strategy
questions.explanation: Retrospective voting is the tendency of voters to base their choices on an evaluation of incumbent officeholders' past performance — rewarding success and punishing failure. V.O. Key's 'The Responsible Electorate' (1966) documented that voters 'vote the pocketbook'; Morris Fiorina formalized the model. Presidential approval ratings and economic conditions (especially GDP growth and unemployment around Election Day) are strong predictors of incumbent party success. AP Gov Unit 5 connects retrospective voting to electoral accountability and contrasts it with prospective voting.

questions.topic: Unit 5: Political Participation · Campaign Strategy
questions.explanation: Prospective voting is choosing candidates based on expectations about what they will do in office — a policy-based, forward-looking model of electoral decision-making. Prospective voters compare candidates' platforms and pick the one closest to their preferred policy position (the 'median voter theorem' predicts candidates converge toward the median voter). AP Gov Unit 5 contrasts prospective voting with retrospective voting; most research suggests voters use both models, with economic retrospection dominating in presidential elections and prospective issue comparison in low-information local races.

The 10th Amendment reserves to the states powers not granted to the federal government. AP Gov Unit 1/2 tests enumerated powers against implied powers (from the Necessary and Proper Clause), and McCulloch v. AP Gov Unit 1 treats limited government as one of the six core constitutional principles, and students often contrast it with the expansive federal authority implied by the Necessary and Proper Clause and the commerce power.

Chief Justice Marshall read 'necessary' broadly in McCulloch v. Maryland (1819) to mean 'useful or appropriate,' dramatically expanding implied federal powers. AP Gov students link the Necessary and Proper Clause to implied powers, McCulloch, and the evolving debate over federal vs. state authority in cases ranging from Gibbons v. Ogden (1824) to Gonzales v. Raich (2005).

Most states allocate all electors winner-take-all. Four times (1824, 1876, 2000, 2016) the winner did not carry the national popular vote. The Connecticut (Great) Compromise created the bicameral design in 1787 to satisfy both large and small states. AP Gov Unit 2 distinguishes House and Senate rules: the Senate allows filibusters and must confirm presidential appointments; the House must originate revenue bills (Article I, Section 7). Both chambers must pass identical legislation before a bill reaches the president.

questions.explanation: Social contract theory holds that individuals surrender some freedoms to government in exchange for protection of their remaining rights, and that government derives its legitimacy from the consent of the governed. Locke's version — that government may be dissolved if it violates the contract — directly shaped the Declaration of Independence's justification for revolution. AP Gov Unit 1 uses social contract theory to explain the philosophical foundations of American democracy; it connects to popular sovereignty, limited government, and the amendment process.

questions.explanation: Selective incorporation is the case-by-case process by which the Supreme Court has applied most provisions of the Bill of Rights to the states through the 14th Amendment's Due Process Clause. Beginning with Gitlow v. New York (1925, free speech) and continuing through McDonald v. Chicago (2010, Second Amendment), most rights are now incorporated. AP Gov Unit 3 uses selective incorporation to explain how federalism and civil liberties interact: the Bill of Rights originally bound only the federal government; incorporation extended it to state and local governments.

questions.explanation: Political socialization is the lifelong process through which individuals acquire their political beliefs, values, and attitudes. Embedded in the Preamble's 'We the People' and operationalized through elections, referenda, and constitutional amendments, it is one of the six core constitutional principles in AP Gov Unit 1. Students connect it to social contract theory, the Declaration of Independence, and the ongoing tension between popular will and constitutional constraints (counter-majoritarian judicial review).

questions.explanation: Public opinion is the distribution of individual attitudes and views about government, politics, and policy across the population. Measured by scientific polling, it influences all branches: presidents track approval ratings to gauge political capital, Congress monitors issue polls before votes, and courts — though formally insulated — sometimes consider broad social consensus. AP Gov Unit 4 covers the formation of public opinion (socialization, media, events), its measurement (random sampling, margin of error), and its translation into policy through elections and interest groups.

questions.explanation: Scientific polling uses probability-based random sampling to estimate population opinion within a calculable margin of error (typically ±3% for 1,000-person national polls). It differs from push polls (designed to change opinion), straw polls (self-selected), and convenience samples (unrepresentative). Gallup's accurate 1936 FDR prediction (vs. Literary Digest's 10-million-person failure) established the power of small representative samples. AP Gov Unit 4 tests students on polling methodology, interpreting results, and evaluating poll quality.

questions.explanation: A political party is an organization seeking to win elections and control government by nominating candidates, mobilizing voters, and organizing governing coalitions. questions.topic: AP Government Final Exam Review · Policy + Elections
questions.explanation: Party realignment is a durable, large-scale shift in partisan loyalties among the electorate, producing a new dominant party coalition and party era. Key examples: the New Deal realignment (1932, Black voters, labor, South to FDR Democrats) and the Southern realignment (1964–1994, white Southerners to Republicans following the Civil Rights Act). AP Gov Unit 5 tests students on realignment vs. dealignment (weakening party ties generally), critical elections as triggers, and the implications for governance and representation.

questions.prompt: AP Government Final Exam Review · Policy + Elections · $300: Senate tactic used to delay or block action.
choices: describing a topic without explaining why it matters
questions.topic: AP Government Final Exam Review · Policy + Elections
questions.explanation: The Senate filibuster allows senators to extend debate indefinitely to delay or block a vote; ending it requires 60 senators to invoke cloture (Rule XXII). It is not in the Constitution and has evolved from actual extended speechmaking to a 'silent filibuster' requiring merely the threat of one. The filibuster was eliminated for executive nominees in 2013 and for SCOTUS nominees in 2017. AP Gov Unit 2 connects it to bicameralism, checks and balances, and polarization — the 60-vote cloture threshold has made bipartisan cooperation a structural legislative requirement.

questions.prompt: AP Government Final Exam Review · Policy + Elections · $400: Election used to select party nominees.
questions.topic: AP Government Final Exam Review · Policy + Elections
questions.explanation: Primary elections allow voters to select party nominees for the general election, replacing the 'smoke-filled room' boss control of nominations. Closed primaries restrict participation to registered party members; open primaries allow any registered voter to participate. Post-1968 McGovern-Fraser reforms democratized presidential primaries, dramatically increasing their number and importance. AP Gov Unit 5 connects primaries to party polarization (activists dominate low-turnout primaries, pulling nominees to extremes), incumbency, and voter participation.

questions.prompt: AP Government Final Exam Review · Policy + Elections · $500: Representative system where voters choose officials to make policy.
questions.topic: AP Government Final Exam Review · Policy + Elections
questions.explanation: Republicanism is the principle that government authority is exercised by elected representatives accountable to the people rather than by direct popular vote. The Founders chose a republic over pure democracy (Federalist No. 10) because they believed a large republic with filtered representation would better control faction and protect minority rights. AP Gov Unit 1 treats republicanism as one of the six core constitutional principles and tests students on its relationship to federalism, the Guarantee Clause (Article IV, Section 4), and the anti-democratic features of constitutional design like the Electoral College and Senate.

questions.prompt: AP Government Final Exam Review · Electoral Accountability · $700: This voting behavior evaluates past government performance.
choices: ignoring the source or data in the prompt
questions.topic: AP Government Final Exam Review · Electoral Accountability
questions.explanation: Retrospective voting is the tendency of voters to reward or punish incumbents based on their past performance in office — most powerfully tied to economic conditions near Election Day ('it's the economy, stupid'). Political scientist Morris Fiorina formalized the model; the 'misery index' (unemployment + inflation) predicted Carter's 1980 loss and George H.W. Bush's 1992 defeat. AP Gov Unit 5 connects retrospective voting to electoral accountability, public opinion, party realignment (voters punish parties in power during downturns), and links all five AP Gov units: constitutional structure → institutional behavior → rights → ideology → participation.
